textPlain: Interesting, i always see attempts to make these types of database tools as super interesting but then I think about all the undocumented edge cases that can come up and they scare me off.Many many years ago I worked on a monitoring tool that itself needed to be highly available, and we needed a solution like this. Ever since that time I've done everything in my power to avoid it.What are the real world cases you built this for? And how can someone like me who has been bruised by past experiences get comfortable with it?
If both nodes approve an update on the same primary key, what happens? I don't see this crucial detail described in the README
how do they resolve write conflict????
You do not want multi-master. If you think you do, think again.Source: I have operated a large multi-master Postgres cluster.
I imagined this position would depend almost entirely on the requirements of the project.  Are you able to elaborate on why it's a universal "NO" for you?
